{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether late-night salivary cortisol is a better screening test for cortisol excess in obese patients with Type 2 diabetes compared to standard screening tests. The documents indicate that late-night salivary cortisol is a preferred initial test for Cushing syndrome due to its convenience and ability to measure free cortisol. It has a sensitivity and specificity of 95% to 98%, which is high. However, the documents also repeatedly mention that obesity is a known cause of false-positive results in screening tests, particularly in pseudo-Cushing syndrome. This includes conditions like obesity, diabetes, and depression, which can mimic hypercortisolism. The dexamethasone suppression test, while commonly used, has a high false-positive rate in obesity. The documents state that late-night salivary cortisol has a higher false-positive rate in obesity compared to other tests. Therefore, despite its convenience and high sensitivity/specificity, it is not superior in obese patients with Type 2 diabetes due to the increased risk of false positives. The standard screening tests, such as the dexamethasone suppression test, are noted to have a high false-positive rate in obesity, but they are still considered standard.
